result:
At dawn, the city looks briefly innocent.

Before the buses begin sighing at curbs, before the first horns lean on their anger, before the sidewalks collect the day’s collisions of ambition and fatigue, there is a pale blue hour when everything seems possible and nothing has yet been ruined. Windows hold squares of gold. Pigeons patrol the gutters with bureaucratic seriousness. Somewhere, a coffee grinder begins its small thunder.

I like to imagine that every morning the world is offered back to us unused.

Not new, exactly. There are still cracks in the pavement, old emails unanswered, dishes in the sink, grief sitting patiently in its chair. But there is a thin mercy in the restart. The mind, if it has slept at all, returns with its pockets emptied. For a few moments, it does not remember every reason to be afraid.

Then it remembers.

This is not a tragedy. It is simply the arrangement. We wake into our lives the way actors walk onstage halfway through a play, expected to know our lines. We improvise. We repeat ourselves. We mistake props for treasures and exits for entrances. Occasionally someone in the audience laughs at the wrong time, and occasionally that saves us.

I once watched a child spend twenty minutes trying to give a leaf to a dog. The dog did not want the leaf. The child could not understand this. The leaf was, by all available measures, excellent: broad, yellow, speckled with brown, shaped like a little flame. The child presented it again and again with increasing urgency. The dog sniffed it, turned away, and looked embarrassed on behalf of everyone.

An adult might have intervened: “Sweetheart, the dog doesn’t want the leaf.” But no one did, and so the ceremony continued until the child finally set the leaf on the ground in front of the dog, as if placing an offering before a god of limited imagination.

I think of that sometimes when I am trying to give the world what it has not asked for.

A poem. An apology. A plan. A version of myself I have polished until it hardly breathes.

The world sniffs, looks away, and waits for something simpler.

Maybe sincerity is mostly the willingness to be ridiculous without immediately defending yourself. To hold out the leaf. To say, “Here. I found this. It seemed beautiful. I thought you might want it.”

There are so many ways to become armored. Sarcasm is armor. Competence can be armor. Taste is often armor—an elegant shield built from preferences. Even despair can be armor, because if nothing matters, nothing can hurt you by failing to matter enough.

Hope is dangerous because it removes the helmet.

And still, people do hopeful things constantly. They plant basil in apartments with no sunlight. They buy notebooks and write “Chapter One.” They adopt old cats. They learn languages they may never need. They text, “Made me think of you.” They stand in front of mirrors and practice saying what they mean.

Every grocery list is an act of faith. So is every calendar. So is washing your sheets.

It is tempting to reserve the word “courage” for grand situations: burning buildings, moral crossroads, journeys across oceans. But most courage is smaller and less cinematic. It is making the phone call. It is entering the room. It is getting out of bed when the day has teeth. It is choosing not to pass your pain along, though it is hot in your hands.

A friend once told me that adulthood is the slow process of becoming someone you would have felt safe with as a child. I have not found a better definition.

Of course, this takes time. We are all haunted houses under renovation. Some rooms are still full of old furniture covered in sheets. Some doors stick. Some nights the pipes bang and we think it is a ghost, when really it is just pressure moving through the walls.

Be patient with the house.

Open windows when you can.

Invite kind people in.

Learn which stair complains and step gently there.

The strange thing is that we are temporary and yet we keep making things that ask to last. Songs. Bridges. Recipes. Names carved into desks. We stack stones against the tide. We save photographs of ordinary afternoons because some part of us knows the ordinary is the first thing time steals.

A bowl of oranges on a table.

A blue towel over a chair.

Someone laughing in the next room.

The dog asleep with one paw twitching.

Nothing much, we say, when asked what happened.

But nothing much is where we live.

If I could offer one small blessing, it would be this: may you notice your life while you are inside it. Not only after. Not only when the room is empty, the season over, the person gone. May you catch yourself in the middle of an unremarkable Tuesday and think, quietly, “I am here. This is happening. There is still time.”

Time for what? I don’t know.

To forgive someone, perhaps. To begin again badly. To drink water. To walk outside and let the weather touch your face. To stop rehearsing disaster. To buy the good bread. To sit beside your own loneliness without treating it like a criminal. To tell the truth, at least to yourself, at least once.

The morning is fully awake now. The city has put on its noise. Somewhere a machine is breaking the street open. Somewhere a nurse is finishing a shift. Somewhere a teenager is pretending not to care. Somewhere a man is carrying flowers and looking both hopeful and doomed.

The pigeons continue their investigations.

The light moves across brick, glass, weeds, traffic, faces.

And the world, which is not new, not innocent, not simple, is nevertheless here again—unfolded, unfinished, holding out its strange and battered hands.

Here, it says.

I found this.

It seemed beautiful.
